questions.q: Let $G$ be a finite simple graph with combinatorial Laplacian $L = D - A$. Which of the following is always true?
choices: $L$ is symmetric but indefinite in general
$L$ is positive semidefinite, but the constant vector is not always in $\ker L$
questions.hint: $L$ is real symmetric, and $f^\top L f = \sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f(i)-f(j))^2 \ge 0$, so PSD.
questions.explain: $L$ is real symmetric, and $f^\top L f = \sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f(i)-f(j))^2 \ge 0$, so PSD. The constant vector $\mathbf{1}$ has $L\mathbf{1}=0$ regardless of the graph, since each row sum of $L$ is $d_i - d_i = 0$.

questions.q: Let $K_5$ be the complete graph on $5$ vertices. What is the largest eigenvalue of the combinatorial Laplacian $L$ of $K_5$?
questions.hint: $L(K_n)$ has eigenvalues $0$ (multiplicity $1$) and $n$ (multiplicity $n-1$).
questions.explain: $L(K_n)$ has eigenvalues $0$ (multiplicity $1$) and $n$ (multiplicity $n-1$). For $n=5$ the largest eigenvalue is $5$.

questions.q: Select all expressions that always equal the Dirichlet energy $f^\top L f$ for any function $f\colon V\to\mathbb{R}$.
questions.hint: Expanding $L=D-A$ gives $f^\top L f = f^\top D f - f^\top A f$, and the standard manipulation rewrites this as $\frac{1}{2}\sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f(i)-f(j))^2$.
questions.explain: Expanding $L=D-A$ gives $f^\top L f = f^\top D f - f^\top A f$, and the standard manipulation rewrites this as $\frac{1}{2}\sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f(i)-f(j))^2$. The unhalved sum (option 3) double-counts each edge.

questions.q: Let $G$ have $n=12$ vertices, and suppose $L_G$ has eigenvalues with $0$ appearing with multiplicity $3$. How many connected components does $G$ have?
questions.hint: The dimension of $\ker L$ equals the number of connected components, so $G$ has exactly $3$ components.
questions.explain: The dimension of $\ker L$ equals the number of connected components, so $G$ has exactly $3$ components.

questions.q: If $f\in\mathbb{R}^V$ satisfies $f^\top L f = 0$, what can be concluded about $f$?
choices: $f$ is constant on the whole graph
$f$ is constant on each connected component (possibly with different constants on different components)
questions.hint: $f^\top L f = \sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f(i)-f(j))^2$.
questions.explain: $f^\top L f = \sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f(i)-f(j))^2$. Vanishing forces $f(i)=f(j)$ across every edge, so $f$ is constant on each component — but components are independent.

questions.q: A graph $G$ on $10$ vertices is the disjoint union of a triangle, a 4-cycle, and three isolated vertices. What is the multiplicity of $0$ as an eigenvalue of $L_G$?
questions.hint: Components: 1 triangle + 1 cycle + 3 isolated vertices = $5$ components, so $\dim\ker L = 5$.
questions.explain: Components: 1 triangle + 1 cycle + 3 isolated vertices = $5$ components, so $\dim\ker L = 5$.

algebraic-connectivity-fiedler.title: Algebraic connectivity and the Fiedler vector

questions.q: What is the Courant–Fischer characterisation of $\lambda_2$ (algebraic connectivity)?
questions.hint: By the variational characterisation, $\lambda_2$ is the minimum of $f^\top L f$ over unit vectors orthogonal to the trivial eigenvector $\mathbf{1}$ (which spans the eigenspace of $\lambda_1=0$ for…
questions.explain: By the variational characterisation, $\lambda_2$ is the minimum of $f^\top L f$ over unit vectors orthogonal to the trivial eigenvector $\mathbf{1}$ (which spans the eigenspace of $\lambda_1=0$ for connected $G$).

questions.q: For which of these graphs is the algebraic connectivity $\lambda_2$ strictly positive?
choices: Two triangles with no edge between them
A cycle $C_4$ with one edge deleted, plus an isolated vertex
questions.hint: $\lambda_2 > 0 \iff G$ is connected.
questions.explain: $\lambda_2 > 0 \iff G$ is connected. The path $P_5$ is connected (so $\lambda_2>0$); the others are disconnected, so $\lambda_2 = 0$.

questions.q: What is the algebraic connectivity $\lambda_2$ of the complete graph $K_4$?
questions.hint: $L(K_n)$ has spectrum $0, n, n, \ldots, n$ ($n-1$ copies of $n$).
questions.explain: $L(K_n)$ has spectrum $0, n, n, \ldots, n$ ($n-1$ copies of $n$). For $K_4$ the second-smallest eigenvalue is $4$.

questions.q: For a $d$-regular connected graph, Cheeger's inequality reads:
questions.hint: Cheeger gives the two-sided bound $\lambda_2/2 \le h(G) \le \sqrt{2 d\, \lambda_2}$.
questions.explain: Cheeger gives the two-sided bound $\lambda_2/2 \le h(G) \le \sqrt{2 d\, \lambda_2}$. The lower bound is easy; the upper bound is the sweep-cut argument and is the source of all spectral approximation algorithms for sparsest cut.

questions.q: Why is the Cheeger upper bound algorithmically useful?
choices: It tells you exactly which subset $S$ achieves $h(G)$
It is constructive: sort vertices by the Fiedler vector, sweep through prefix cuts, and the best is within a factor $\sqrt{2d/\lambda_2}$ of optimal
It tells you that $h(G)$ can be computed in $O(n)$ time exactly
questions.hint: Computing $h(G)$ exactly is NP-hard.
questions.explain: Computing $h(G)$ exactly is NP-hard. Cheeger's upper bound is constructive — the sweep-cut algorithm — and gives a polynomial-time approximation factor of $\sqrt{2d/\lambda_2}$, the prototype for spectral approximation.

questions.q: Select the consequences that follow from Cheeger's inequality $\lambda_2/2 \le h(G) \le \sqrt{2 d \lambda_2}$.
questions.hint: Both directions are equivalent characterisations of disconnection (item 1).
questions.explain: Both directions are equivalent characterisations of disconnection (item 1). Item 2 follows from the lower bound; item 3 from the upper bound. Item 4 is a wrong rearrangement: the lower bound is $\lambda_2/2 \le h(G)$, not $\sqrt{\lambda_2} \le h(G)$.

random-walks-mixing.title: Random walks and the spectral gap

questions.q: On a connected non-bipartite $d$-regular graph, what is the stationary distribution of the simple random walk?
questions.hint: For a $d$-regular graph all $d_i = d$, so $\pi_i = d_i/(2|E|) = d/(nd) = 1/n$.
questions.explain: For a $d$-regular graph all $d_i = d$, so $\pi_i = d_i/(2|E|) = d/(nd) = 1/n$. The general formula is $\pi_i = d_i/(2|E|)$, which specialises to uniform in the regular case.

questions.q:
What is the spectral gap controlling mixing on a non-bipartite graph, in terms of the normalised Laplacian eigenvalues $0=
u_1\le
u_2\le\cdots\le
u_n$?
questions.hint: The transition matrix $P$ has eigenvalues $1-\nu_k\in[-1,1]$.
questions.explain:
The transition matrix $P$ has eigenvalues $1-
u_k\in[-1,1]$. The slowest non-trivial decay is the largest of $|1-
u_2|=1-
u_2$ and $|1-
u_n|=
u_n-1$, so the spectral gap is $1$ minus that maximum, namely $\min(
u_2,\,2-
u_n)$.

questions.q: Select all reasons a random walk on a graph can mix slowly.
choices: $\nu_n$ is close to $2$ (graph is nearly bipartite)
The graph has high maximum degree
questions.hint: Slow mixing comes from a small spectral gap, which in turn means either $\nu_2 \approx 0$ (Cheeger / near-disconnection) or $\nu_n \approx 2$ (near-bipartite, parity oscillation).
questions.explain: Slow mixing comes from a small spectral gap, which in turn means either $\nu_2 \approx 0$ (Cheeger / near-disconnection) or $\nu_n \approx 2$ (near-bipartite, parity oscillation). Triangle counts and degree alone do not dictate mixing speed.

questions.q: For a connected graph $G$, the largest normalised Laplacian eigenvalue $\nu_n$ equals $2$ if and only if:
questions.hint: $\nu_n = 2 \iff G$ is bipartite.
questions.explain: $\nu_n = 2 \iff G$ is bipartite. The corresponding eigenvector is $D^{1/2}\mathbf{1}_L - D^{1/2}\mathbf{1}_R$ for the bipartition $V = L\sqcup R$.

questions.q: What does the Alon–Boppana bound say?
choices: For any infinite family of $d$-regular graphs, $\liminf \lambda_2(A) \ge 2\sqrt{d-1}$
$\lambda_2(A)$ can be made arbitrarily close to $0$ in a regular family
questions.hint: Alon–Boppana: any infinite family of $d$-regular graphs has $\liminf_n \lambda_2(A_{G_n}) \ge 2\sqrt{d-1}$.
questions.explain: Alon–Boppana: any infinite family of $d$-regular graphs has $\liminf_n \lambda_2(A_{G_n}) \ge 2\sqrt{d-1}$. Graphs that achieve $|\lambda_2(A)| \le 2\sqrt{d-1}$ are called Ramanujan — they essentially saturate this bound.

questions.q: Which of the following are properties of an expander family $(G_n)$ of $d$-regular graphs?
choices: Random walks mix in $O(\log n)$ steps
The graph is a complete graph
questions.hint: An expander is sparse ($dn/2$ edges) and has a uniform spectral gap, which (via Cheeger and the mixing-time bound) yields $O(\log n)$ mixing time.
questions.explain: An expander is sparse ($dn/2$ edges) and has a uniform spectral gap, which (via Cheeger and the mixing-time bound) yields $O(\log n)$ mixing time. Complete graphs $K_n$ have great mixing but are not sparse — they are not expanders in the asymptotic sense.

questions.q: In spectral clustering with $k$ clusters, which eigenvectors of the (normalised) Laplacian are used as embedding coordinates?
choices: The eigenvectors $u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_k$ for the $k$ smallest eigenvalues
The eigenvectors $u_{n-k+1}, \ldots, u_n$ for the $k$ largest eigenvalues
questions.hint: The standard recipe (Shi–Malik / Ng–Jordan–Weiss): take the eigenvectors at the bottom $k$ eigenvalues.
questions.explain: The standard recipe (Shi–Malik / Ng–Jordan–Weiss): take the eigenvectors at the bottom $k$ eigenvalues. Each row of the resulting $n\times k$ matrix is a vertex's coordinates in $\mathbb{R}^k$, then $k$-means is run on the rows.

questions.q: Why does spectral clustering recover well-separated communities?
choices: Eigenvectors of large eigenvalues capture noise

When $G$ is exactly $k$ disjoint components, the bottom-$k$ eigenspace is spanned by the indicator vectors of components; with weakly-coupled clusters the eigenvectors are a small perturbation thereof
$k$-means is the only clustering algorithm that works on graphs
questions.hint: When the graph is the disjoint union of $k$ components, $\dim\ker L = k$ and component indicators span the kernel.
questions.explain: When the graph is the disjoint union of $k$ components, $\dim\ker L = k$ and component indicators span the kernel. Adding a few cross-cluster edges perturbs these eigenvectors only slightly, so the embedding still maps each cluster to a tight blob — exactly what $k$-means picks up.

questions.q: What does a large eigengap $\nu_{k+1} - \nu_k$ in the normalised Laplacian heuristically suggest?
questions.hint: The eigengap heuristic: a large gap after the $k$-th eigenvalue indicates that the bottom $k$ eigenvalues form a coherent group close to $0$, signalling a $k$-cluster structure.
questions.explain: The eigengap heuristic: a large gap after the $k$-th eigenvalue indicates that the bottom $k$ eigenvalues form a coherent group close to $0$, signalling a $k$-cluster structure. It is heuristic, not a theorem, but remarkably reliable.
